best_for: Fast component/theme discovery for React + Tailwind + shadcn/ui projects; community-published components with live preview and an install CLI (npx shadcn@latest add "https://21st.dev/r/..."); also has an AI prompt-to-component mode.
avoid_when: Treating a published component as production-audited -- anyone can publish; check the code before shipping, especially for a11y and unused deps.
tradeoffs: Huge selection and fast to browse, but quality/maintenance varies by author; always WebFetch the specific component page for current code rather than assuming an install command from memory.

best_for: Turning a text/image prompt into a working React + shadcn/ui + Tailwind component or page fast, especially for first-draft layouts.
avoid_when: Shipping v0 output unreviewed to a client -- generated code can carry redundant state, missing accessibility attributes, or extra dependencies.
tradeoffs: Fastest path from idea to code; output quality depends on prompt specificity, and it's a paid/credit-metered tool above the free tier -- check current pricing before assuming cost.

best_for: The foundation most other libraries in this file build on -- copy-paste-owned (not an npm black box) accessible Radix-based primitives (button, dialog, dropdown, form, etc.) styled with Tailwind.
avoid_when: Needing a fully-themed marketing page out of the box -- shadcn/ui itself is deliberately unopinionated/minimal; pair with a blocks or animation library above for that.
tradeoffs: Because code lands in your repo (not node_modules), you fully own and can restyle every component -- but that also means you own keeping it patched; re-run the CLI to pull updates.

best_for: Official, maintained full-page sections (dashboards, login, sidebar, calendar) built from shadcn/ui primitives -- highest confidence 'copy-paste and it just works' option in the ecosystem.
avoid_when: Assuming the block matches your brand's Design DNA out of the box -- these are intentionally neutral; re-skin tokens (color/type/radius) before shipping as final.
tradeoffs: Official maintenance and consistent quality, but selection is smaller and more generic-looking than third-party block marketplaces -- combine with frontend-distinctiveness.md guidance to avoid a templated look.

best_for: Large paid marketplace of full marketing/app page blocks (hero, pricing, testimonial, footer, etc.) built on shadcn/ui, for teams that want breadth without building each section from scratch.
avoid_when: Assuming it's free -- this is a paid marketplace; confirm current license/seat terms live before quoting a client a cost.
tradeoffs: Much broader block selection than the official shadcn/ui blocks page, at a licensing cost and with more third-party maintenance risk.

best_for: High-motion marketing/landing sections (parallax heroes, animated backgrounds, 3D card hovers) built with Framer Motion + Tailwind, when the brand wants a 'wow' first impression.
avoid_when: Using it for a dense application UI (dashboards, forms) -- it's tuned for marketing pages, not information-dense screens; heavy animation there hurts usability.
tradeoffs: Strong visual distinctiveness out of the box, but animation-heavy components add bundle weight and can hurt Core Web Vitals (INP/CLS) if overused -- budget which sections actually need motion.

best_for: Similar animated-marketing-component niche to Aceternity UI (particles, marquees, animated text/highlight effects) -- good for A/B-ing visual style against Aceternity for the same brief.
avoid_when: Mixing too many animation libraries' components on one page -- inconsistent motion timing/easing reads as unpolished; pick one animation vocabulary per project.
tradeoffs: Free tier covers a solid core set; some components are pro-tier -- verify current free/paid split live before committing to one in a client scope.

best_for: Smaller, more composable Framer Motion + Tailwind animation primitives (text reveal, morphing dialog, image comparison) when you want to build a custom animated component rather than take a full pre-built section.
avoid_when: Needing a complete page section immediately -- this is primitive-level, not block-level; expect to compose several of these into a section yourself.
tradeoffs: More flexible/composable than Aceternity or Magic UI's full sections, at the cost of more assembly work.

best_for: Zero-cost, framework-agnostic Tailwind HTML blocks (marketing sections and application UI) when there's no budget for a paid kit and no need for React-specific interactivity.
avoid_when: Needing built-in interactivity (dropdown state, modal open/close) -- these are markup-only; you wire up behavior yourself (Alpine.js, React state, etc.).
tradeoffs: Completely free with no attribution required, but plain markup means more integration work than a component-library option with built-in JS behavior.

best_for: Large free catalog of shadcn/ui-based components with more variety than the official blocks page, when budget is zero but you still want the shadcn/ui ownership model.
avoid_when: Expecting official-maintainer-level long-term support -- it's community/independent-maintained; check activity/last-update before depending on it for a long-lived client project.
tradeoffs: Free and shadcn/ui-compatible (copy-paste ownership), but wider variety than the official blocks page means more quality variance component-to-component.

best_for: Free, large application-focused Tailwind block library (forms, tables, navs, dashboards) -- good default when the project needs a lot of app-chrome UI fast and isn't shadcn/ui-based.
avoid_when: Building a marketing-first landing page -- Preline leans toward application/admin UI over marketing polish; pair with an animation library above for that instead.
tradeoffs: Deep application-UI coverage for free, but visual style is more generic/utilitarian than animation-first options -- expect to add brand personality on top.

best_for: Free Tailwind components with first-class RTL support built in -- the strongest option here when the project needs Arabic/Hebrew/Persian layout support out of the box.
avoid_when: Needing React-specific interactive state -- like Hyperui, this is markup-focused; wire up behavior separately.
tradeoffs: Best RTL coverage of the free-kit options, narrower total component count than Hyperui/Origin UI.

best_for: Another free Tailwind block/component catalog -- useful as a third opinion to compare against Hyperui/Origin UI for the same section when neither quite matches the brief.
avoid_when: Relying on it as the sole source for a large build -- smaller catalog and community than Hyperui/Preline; better as a supplement.
tradeoffs: Free and easy to browse, but the smallest catalog of the free-kit group listed here.

best_for: Projects that want semantic Tailwind component classnames (btn, card, modal) plus a built-in theme-switcher system, framework-agnostic (works with plain HTML, React, Vue, Svelte alike).
avoid_when: Wanting fully bespoke, highly distinctive visuals straight out of the box -- daisyUI's default themes are recognizable; treat them as a starting point to restyle, per this skill's anti-generic-AI-design rule.
tradeoffs: Fastest way to get a consistent, themeable component system with minimal markup, but the class-based approach is a different mental model than copy-paste JSX components (shadcn/ui-style) -- pick one approach per project, don't mix.

best_for: Tailwind component library with official React/Vue/Svelte/Angular bindings plus a free Figma design system -- good when the team wants matching design + code across multiple frameworks.
avoid_when: Assuming every block is free -- Flowbite has a paid Pro block library on top of the free core; verify which tier a given block belongs to before scoping.
tradeoffs: Broad framework support and a matching Figma kit is unusual among these options, at the cost of a less distinctive default visual style -- reskin before shipping.

best_for: A full React component library (not copy-paste markup) with built-in dark mode, accessibility, and animation via Tailwind Variants + Framer Motion -- good when the team prefers npm install + import over owning component source.
avoid_when: Wanting full control over every line of component code -- as an installed package (not copy-paste), customization goes through its theming/variant API rather than direct edits; if that's a blocker, prefer shadcn/ui instead.
tradeoffs: Faster initial setup and consistent a11y/dark-mode behavior out of the box, but less ownership/customization depth than the copy-paste (shadcn/ui-family) libraries above.

best_for: shadcn/ui-based components with more interactive/animated flourishes than the official blocks page (e.g. direction-aware hover, dynamic islands) -- a middle ground between plain shadcn/ui and full animation-first libraries.
avoid_when: Needing the largest possible component count -- smaller, more curated catalog than Origin UI or Shadcnblocks.com.
tradeoffs: Well-curated and shadcn/ui-compatible (owns code in-repo), but the smaller catalog means more gaps to fill from other sources on a full build.

best_for: Purpose-built React component library for dashboards and analytics UI -- charts, KPI cards, data tables -- when the project's core UI problem is presenting data, not marketing. The core component set (35+ components) is free and open-source; a separate premium block library (300+ templates) also exists on top of it.
avoid_when: Building a marketing/landing page -- Tremor is scoped to dashboard/data-viz UI specifically, not general-purpose sections.
tradeoffs: Far less assembly work than hand-rolling charts with a raw charting library, but narrower scope than the general-purpose libraries above -- pair with shadcn/ui or Preline for the surrounding app chrome.

option: Need: fastest to ship, no design decisions left to make
best_for: shadcn/ui Blocks or Shadcnblocks.com (official/paid full pages) first; fall back to 21st.dev search for a specific missing section.
avoid_when: Skipping the reskin step because it shipped fast -- 'fast' and 'distinctive' are different goals; still run it past frontend-distinctiveness.md.
tradeoffs: Speed over distinctiveness by default -- explicitly flag this tradeoff to the user before choosing this path for brand-forward work.

best_for: Aceternity UI or Magic UI for full sections, Motion Primitives if you need to compose something custom from smaller pieces.
avoid_when: The brand's Design DNA calls for restraint (e.g. luxury-minimal, corporate-clean styles) -- heavy motion actively fights those aesthetics; check style-vocabulary.csv first.
tradeoffs: High visual impact for cost of bundle size and Core Web Vitals risk (INP/CLS) -- test performance after integrating, not just before.

option: Need: zero budget, framework-agnostic markup only
best_for: Hyperui, Origin UI, Meraki UI (RTL), or Mamba UI, in that rough order of catalog size -- Origin UI if already committed to shadcn/ui's ownership model, Hyperui otherwise.
avoid_when: The project needs built-in JS interactivity (dropdowns, modals with state) -- these ship markup only; budget the wiring-up time.
tradeoffs: No license cost, but more integration labor than a framework component library with built-in behavior.

best_for: Tremor for charts/KPI cards + shadcn/ui Blocks or Preline for the surrounding sidebar/table/form chrome.
avoid_when: Reaching for an animation-first library (Aceternity/Magic UI) here -- motion competes with scanability in dense data UI; keep dashboards calm per the data-dense-dashboard style guidance.
tradeoffs: Combining two libraries (data-viz + app chrome) covers the full screen but means reconciling two different token/theming systems -- normalize colors/spacing after integration.

best_for: Flowbite (official Vue/Svelte/Angular bindings) or daisyUI (framework-agnostic classnames) -- most of the other libraries in this file are React/shadcn-specific.
avoid_when: Assuming a shadcn/ui-ecosystem library (21st.dev, Aceternity, Origin UI, Cult UI) will port cleanly to Vue/Svelte -- they're React-only; don't promise portability without checking.
tradeoffs: Narrower selection than the React-first ecosystem, but avoids a framework mismatch mid-build.

option: Need: fast first-draft from a prompt/sketch, will hand-refine after
best_for: v0.dev for a prompt-to-code first pass, or 21st.dev's AI mode -- both output shadcn/ui-compatible React, so the result still fits the rest of this file's ecosystem.
avoid_when: Shipping the first-pass output as final -- always run it through the testing/a11y checklist and a manual design review before calling it done; generated code is a draft, not a deliverable.
tradeoffs: Big head start over a blank page, but requires disciplined human review after -- budget that review time in the estimate, don't treat AI generation as free.
